parsed_findings.chain_steps: Steal the plaintext access_token from SharedPreferences, then replay it to authenticate as the victim.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker who can access the stored preferences can steal the access token and impersonate the user or access protected APIs.
parsed_findings.proof: p.edit().putString('access_token', token).apply() writes the token directly to SharedPreferences.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call saveToken with any access token, then extract the app's SharedPreferences XML (e.g., via backup/rooted device/tools) to read the access_token value.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The code stores a sensitive access token in plaintext in Android SharedPreferences without encryption or secure storage protections.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypassing the guard with `out == NULL` leads directly to the NULL dereference in `memcpy`.
parsed_findings.impact: This can be exploited to cause a denial of service via a NULL pointer dereference.
parsed_findings.proof: Because the check returns only when `out` is non-NULL, a call with `out == NULL` and `out_sz > 0` will reach `memcpy(out, in, n)` (line 5) and dereference a NULL pointer.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Invoke `copy_name(in, NULL, 2)` with `in` pointing to a valid string and `out_sz > 0`, which will crash when executing `memcpy(out, in, n)`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The input validation uses `if (out || out_sz == 0)` which incorrectly rejects valid `out` pointers and, more importantly, allows `out == NULL` to pass and then dereferences it in `memcpy`.
